title: From Self To Others
study: A Study In The Life Of Moses
scriptureForWeek: EXODUS 2:11-15 Many years later, when Moses had grown up, he went out to visit his own people, the Hebrews, and he saw how hard they were forced to work. During his visit, he saw an Egyptian beating one of his fellow Hebrews. After looking in all directions to make sure no one was watching, Moses killed the Egyptian and hid the body in the sand. The next day, when Moses went out to visit his people again, he saw two Hebrew men fighting. “Why are you beating up your friend?” Moses said to the one who had started the fight. The man replied, “Who appointed you to be our prince and judge? Are you going to kill me as you killed that Egyptian yesterday?” Then Moses was afraid, thinking, “Everyone knows what I did.” And sure enough, Pharaoh heard what had happened, and he tried to kill Moses. But Moses fled from Pharaoh and went to live in the land of Midian.
weekTheme: Boys ignore, Men see All good stories have a moment near the beginning of the story called the inciting incident. This is the moment the lead character is pulled out of his comfortable life and invited into a risky adventure. This is the moment the call is given and the character has to respond. This week we look at the moment when God wakes Moses up from his comfortable life, and he sees the needs of others for the first time. This is Moses’ inciting incident for the call of God on his life. He will awaken from a life of privilege and comfort in the palace to seeing the slavery and oppression of his own people. His eyes will open, his heart will break, and his worldview will change. He will act decisively and things will never be the same again. God wants to call us out of our comfort and ease and wake us up to his calling to serve others in the world. This can be a painful and hard journey but it is essential to becoming a man. Boys ignore the needs of the world, men see and respond.

days.title: Overcoming The "Me Monster"
days.scripture: EXODUS 2:11 Many years later, when Moses had grown up, he went out to visit his own people, the Hebrews, and he saw how hard they were forced to work.
days.reflection: The world does not revolve around you. Sounds kind of abrupt, but it’s true. The sooner you embrace that reality, the healthier your life will be. That’s not to say that your life isn’t important, or that you don’t have an important place in the world, but it’s to help you learn to look beyond yourself to the needs of others. In 2022, the world’s population surpassed 8 billion people. 8 billion of us share this tiny little planet hanging in the middle of space. To live well and become a wise and godly man, you are going to have to care about people other than yourself. Child development psychologists tell us that a child under 2 cannot comprehend the reality that the world does not revolve around them. They cannot think about anything that is outside of their immediate view or a world that meaningfully includes others. Sometimes you can revert to acting like a toddler when you’re a teenager. If you don’t like something or someone, you block them out of your reality. The struggle to care about others when growing up even has a name. It’s called adolescent egocentrism. Now that is a large and clunky term. What on earth could that have to do with someone like you? Adolescent egocentrism was developed by a child psychologist named Dr. David Elkind. He studied young people ranging from ages 11 to 18, focusing on how they perceived the world compared to adults. Dr. Elkind discovered that teenagers were basically unable to tell the difference between their own perceptions and the perceptions of others. Teenagers consistently believed their view was the only possible view and that other people’s perspectives didn’t matter. An easier way to remember this is the name “Me Monster.” The Me Monster lives in each one of us and just thinks about itself. Me, me, me, me, me. My, my, my, my, my. I, I, I, I, I. It’s hard to overcome the Me Monster and care about others. It’s a real war. But God is committed to helping us learn to grow up and love and serve others. God will use events, conversations, and people to silence the Me Monster and wake us up to love and care about other people. For Moses, this happened on a simple trip to visit his own people. Moses has been living in a palace, in luxury and privilege. He has been getting an incredible education and mainly enjoying himself. One day, everything changed. He goes to visit his own people, the Hebrews, and has a revelation. While he has been living the good life, they have been enslaved in horrible circumstances. They were being forced to build the empire of Pharaoh, against their will. Moses had an awakening that opened his eyes and mind. He could no longer enjoy the palace while his people suffered like this. He went from a focus on himself to a focus on others. This is a shift that must happen in the heart of every man. This is a shift God will work in us as we grow older and mature into men. God is going to help you outgrow adolescent egocentrism. He is going to help you tame the Me Monster. He is going to open your eyes to the needs of others in a powerful way. So, keep your eyes open. Pay attention to others. Be curious, be compassionate. You never know when the shift is going to happen. A shift from self to others, from thinking like a boy to loving like a man. Lean in, you are already on your way.
questions: Do you think the culture we live in is helping us become more or less selfish? Can you give some examples of your opinion?
Have you ever had a moment when you were moved with compassion for others? What was it? How did it affect you?
Ask the person you are doing this study with about a key moment that really woke him up to the needs of others. How old was he and what was it?

thoughts.verse: He died for everyone so that those who receive his new life will no longer live for themselves. Instead, they will live for Christ, who died and was raised for them.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a heart to care for others this week. Ask him to help you to wrestle the Me Monster down with God's love and care.

days.title: Compelled By Compassion
days.scripture: EXODUS 2:11 Many years later, when Moses had grown up, he went out to visit his own people, the Hebrews, and he saw how hard they were forced to work. During his visit, he saw an Egyptian beating one of his fellow Hebrews.
days.reflection: There are lots of ways to change people’s behavior. You can guilt someone, nag them, threaten them, reward them, punish them, etc. These are all outside pressures designed to get someone to act differently. But when the pressure is off, people often revert back to what they really think in their hearts. I’m sure you’ve experienced this yourself. You do something so you don’t get in trouble, but it doesn’t change what you really think and feel in your heart. This is important to understand because the shift that God is trying to make in your heart is not by pressuring you to do stuff you don’t want to. He is trying to change your heart so you learn to want the right things. God isn’t wanting to force you to care for others, he is working to change your heart so you do care for others. According to St. Augustine (a really cool bishop who lived about 1700 years ago), sin doesn’t show up in our lives by making us go crazy, break all the rules, and defy God. Sin is mainly about collapsing inward like a black hole of selfishness. He called this “Incurvatus”, love turned in on itself. Incurvatus sounds a bit like a villain from a Marvel movie, and in many ways it is. Sin is a villain that always makes us think about ourselves. But God will change our hearts to love others. He will fill us with compassion. And that is an inward work that leads to outward actions. We become a well of love, not a black hole of selfishness. This happens by God putting his love in our hearts. Romans 5:5 says, “For we know how dearly God loves us, because he has given us the Holy Spirit to fill our hearts with his love.” The Spirit fills our hearts with God's love, then our hearts want to love others. Moses experienced something new in his heart. Compassion. He felt connected to the suffering of his people for the first time. The word compassion means a “Deep awareness of the suffering of another accompanied by the wish to relieve it.” He could no longer live his life knowing the people he was connected to were suffering. He woke up, he cared, and he had to do something. He was pulled out of his own selfish story into the story of others. That’s what love does. It breaks the gravity of self and pulls us into a world of love. Compassion is when our hearts want to help others, not because we have to, but because we want to. Jesus was filled with compassion in an even greater way than Moses was. Jesus came to save us, not because he had to - none of us deserved his mercy - but because of his love. He was moved with compassion. Check it out. Matthew 14:14 - Jesus saw the huge crowd as he stepped from the boat, and he had compassion on them and healed their sick. Mark 1:40-41 - A man with leprosy came and knelt in front of Jesus, begging to be healed. “If you are willing, you can heal me and make me clean,” he said. Moved with compassion, Jesus reached out and touched him. “I am willing,” he said. “Be healed!” Mark 6:34 - Jesus saw the huge crowd as he stepped from the boat, and he had compassion on them because they were like sheep without a shepherd. So he began teaching them many things. Luke 7:13 - When the Lord saw her, his heart overflowed with compassion. “Don’t cry!” he said. Shifting from self to others is a shift from selfishness to compassion. It’s a shift powered by God's work in our hearts. It wasn’t guilt or shame that opened Moses’ eyes, but love in his heart, love given by God. It will be the same for you. Your teachers can lecture you, your friends pressure you, your parents nag you, but, ultimately, it will be God who changes you. Why don’t you ask him to do that today?
questions: Who is the most compassionate person you know? How do they show this?
Have you ever seen God change a person’s heart so they act differently? If so, who, and how did they change?
Who is someone in your world that you think could use some compassion?
What is one practical way you could show compassion to that person today?

thoughts.verse: We know what real love is because Jesus gave up his life for us. So we also ought to give up our lives for our brothers and sisters. If someone has enough money to live well and sees a brother or sister in need but shows no compassion—how can God’s love be in that person?
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment and ask God to fill your heart with his love and compassion. You may want to pray something like this. “Lord, your word says that you spread the Father's love in people's hearts through the power of the Holy Spirit. Holy Spirit, would you fill me with love and compassion today. Would you help me see others as you do. Would you bring me out of myself and into the world of others, just as Jesus entered our world to care for us. In Jesus’ name. Amen.

days.title: Seeing Others
days.scripture: EXODUS 2:11-15 11 Many years later, when Moses had grown up, he went out to visit his own people, the Hebrews, and he saw how hard they were forced to work. During his visit, he saw an Egyptian beating one of his fellow Hebrews. 12 After looking in all directions to make sure no one was watching, Moses killed the Egyptian and hid the body in the sand. 13 The next day, when Moses went out to visit his people again, he saw two Hebrew men fighting. “Why are you beating up your friend?” Moses said to the one who had started the fight. 14 The man replied, “Who appointed you to be our prince and judge? Are you going to kill me as you killed that Egyptian yesterday?” Then Moses was afraid, thinking, “Everyone knows what I did.” 15 And sure enough, Pharaoh heard what had happened, and he tried to kill Moses. But Moses fled from Pharaoh and went to live in the land of Midian.
days.reflection: One of the best feelings in the world is being seen. Not just with your eyes, like “there you are.” But when others see you as you really are. When they know what you like, what you struggle with, where you need encouragement, what your favorite things are. When they take the time to make you feel special, valued and loved. People in our world today ache to be seen. So many guys your age feel lonely, forgotten, overlooked, and left out. God is always trying to get us to see the world the way he does, with a passion to help those who are hurting. But there can be a war for our attention. There are so many distractions. So many algorithms designed to get us to focus on ourselves. That’s why we need to pay attention to who God may want to help through us. Simone Weil said, “Attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity.” In a war for attention, focusing on others is a weapon of love. Being generous isn’t just about giving money or possession to others; generosity is choosing to give them some of our attention. That’s why Proverbs 21:13 says, “Those who shut their ears to the cries of the poor will be ignored in their own time of need.” Proverbs 28:27 also says, “Whoever gives to the poor will lack nothing, but those who close their eyes to poverty will be cursed." We can close our eyes to the poverty we see around us if we choose, but we often will close our hearts to God's love. Exodus 3:7 says, “Then the LORD told him, ‘I have certainly seen the oppression of my people in Egypt. I have heard their cries of distress because of their harsh slave drivers. Yes, I am aware of their suffering.’" God saw the Israelites suffering; God heard their cries. And he wanted Moses to as well. He was looking for someone to care about others the way he did, someone he could use to deliver his people. To look beyond yourself, and see and hear the cries of others is at the heart of becoming a man. Martin Luther King Jr said, “Life’s most urgent question is: ‘What are you doing for others?’” Doing things for others makes you masculine. Focusing on others makes you a man. Moses’ whole life changed when he shifted his focus from self to others. From “me” to “we.” It opened his heart and life to an adventure of love, miracles, pain, and wonder he never could have imagined. I sometimes wonder what would have happened if Moses had closed his ears and shut his eyes to the needs of his people. What would have happened if Moses went home and ate a pizza and read a story because what he saw was too emotionally intense? What would have happened if he had said, “those poor people, I'll pray for them.” But kept on living his life as a prince in the palace for himself? How would anything have changed if he decided to just pop into the slave pits once a year to give out food at Christmas? He would have missed his calling. Millions would have suffered, and he would have wasted all the preparation God had done so far. God wants to use you, yes - you, to care about the needs of others. He wants you to learn to tune your eyes and ears to see people the way he does and respond with his love. He wants you to see the world through the eyes of Jesus, the one moved with compassion. Open your eyes today with a new perspective and see who needs love. Open your ears today and hear the cries of people behind the things they say. God may do something amazing in your heart if you just tune in.
questions: Can you think of a time recently when you felt truly “seen?” Like someone really understood what you were going through and cared for you? Would you be willing to share about it?
What are some of the ways our culture stops guys your age from seeing and hearing others?
Think about the stories of Jesus in the bible. Can you think of a moment when Jesus sees and hears other people’s pain when no one else noticed?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share his favorite moment Jesus noticed those others overlooked.

thoughts.verse: Don’t be selfish; don’t try to impress others. Be humble, thinking of others as better than yourselves. Don’t look out only for your own interests, but take an interest in others, too.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment and ask God to open your eyes to see others differently today. Ask him to open your ears to hear what people may be struggling with in a fresh way. And then thank him that he sees and hears the deepest things happening in your heart.

days.title: Step In And Stand Up
days.scripture: EXODUS 2:11-12 11 Many years later, when Moses had grown up, he went out to visit his own people, the Hebrews, and he saw how hard they were forced to work. During his visit, he saw an Egyptian beating one of his fellow Hebrews. 12 After looking in all directions to make sure no one was watching, Moses killed the Egyptian and hid the body in the sand.
days.reflection: One of the classic plots people love in a movie is “the bully, the victim, and the hero.” You know how this goes. A bully comes along and starts picking on someone. The victim doesn’t have the power to defend themselves. This happens day after day until a hero comes along who jumps into the story. He likes the victim, can’t stand the bully, and then stands between them. Now, the cycle is disrupted. The bully turns his anger on the hero. Then the hero is faced with a choice. Will he have the courage to step up and act? Is he willing to get into a fight for the sake of others? Can he confront his fears, silence his selfishness, and stand up for what is right? The hero wrestles to overcome his inner fears and eventually confronts the bully. A fight ensues, the bully is defeated, and the victim is empowered. The hero and the victim become friends, and life goes on. That plot line is a classic, and it never fails to move something deep in the heart of young men. We long to stand up for what is right. We ache to be strong enough to defend others. We long to show heroic love to those we care about. This longing comes from God. Men are at their best when they use their strength to help people, not harm them. The story of Moses follows this plot in many ways. Moses has an awakening that confronts him with a choice. His eyes are opened to the oppression of his people. He realizes that he is not Egyptian, but a Hebrew. He is not from a royal family, but an oppressed one. He is not on the winning team, but the losing one. And now he has a choice. Will he keep living for himself, or act on behalf of the oppressed? Moses does act. He sees a fellow Hebrew being beaten by a slave master and he steps in. He stops the beating, kills the man, and a choice is made. Moses will be a man who cares about his people, not one who turns away or cowers back. The New Testament comments on Moses’ choice here. Hebrews 11:24-26 says, “By faith Moses, when he had grown up, refused to be known as the son of Pharaoh’s daughter. He chose to be mistreated along with the people of God rather than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a short time. He regarded disgrace for the sake of Christ as of greater value than the treasures of Egypt, because he was looking ahead to his reward.” These are strong words here. Refused. Chose. Regarded. Moses acts on behalf of others. He steps up. Moses has started a fight with the bully. Little does he know how big this fight will eventually be. You too will have to learn to wrestle with your fears and stand up for others. You will have to learn to step up. You will have to overcome the selfishness, self-preservation, and struggle inside you. But God will help you. It will take time, and you may make some mistakes along the way, but keep moving forward. This is a part of the painful and messy process of growing into a man. This is a part of becoming like Jesus. Jesus stepped up and confronted the ultimate bully, Satan. He got between us and our sin. He wrestled to do the Father's will in the garden and surrendered to love. John 15:13 says this, “There is no greater love than to lay down one’s life for one’s friends.” Jesus laid down his life for us. He acted. He stepped in and stepped up. He died for our sin, rose again in power, and sits at the right hand of God. Standing up for others is a godly instinct. It needs to be turned into godly action. God wants to use you to help others. Step in, stand up.
questions: Can you think of a movie you like that has the “bully, hero, victim” plot line? What about it do you connect with?
Have you ever had a moment when you felt like you were meant to stick up for someone but you didn’t? Without any shame, can you describe what happened in your heart that made you think you couldn’t?
Ask the person you are doing this study with about a time they had to “step in and stand up” for someone else. Who was it, and what happened?

thoughts.verse: What good is it, dear brothers and sisters, if you say you have faith but don’t show it by your actions? Can that kind of faith save anyone? Suppose you see a brother or sister who has no food or clothing, and you say, “Good-bye and have a good day; stay warm and eat well”—but then you don’t give that person any food or clothing. What good does that do? So you see, faith by itself isn’t enough. Unless it produces good deeds, it is dead and useless.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you from it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you courage in your heart. Ask him for strength to step in and stand up for others today. Thank him that Jesus did this for you.

days.title: God'S Things, God'S Way
days.scripture: EXODUS 2:11-15 11 Many years later, when Moses had grown up, he went out to visit his own people, the Hebrews, and he saw how hard they were forced to work. During his visit, he saw an Egyptian beating one of his fellow Hebrews. 12 After looking in all directions to make sure no one was watching, Moses killed the Egyptian and hid the body in the sand. 13 The next day, when Moses went out to visit his people again, he saw two Hebrew men fighting. “Why are you beating up your friend?” Moses said to the one who had started the fight. 14 The man replied, “Who appointed you to be our prince and judge? Are you going to kill me as you killed that Egyptian yesterday?” Then Moses was afraid, thinking, “Everyone knows what I did.” 15 And sure enough, Pharaoh heard what had happened, and he tried to kill Moses. But Moses fled from Pharaoh and went to live in the land of Midian.
days.reflection: Have you ever tried to do the right thing, but done it the wrong way? Have you ever been in a situation that made you so angry you acted rashly and later wished you could have controlled yourself? Almost all guys have done this. It’s really hard when we are angry to figure out how to channel that anger in the proper direction. It’s hard to think rationally when you are mad! When we are angry, we can act out of good motives and still mess stuff up. Moses did this. What he saw infuriated him. The passage today has some heavy words in it. The Israelites were “forced to work.” One of the Egyptians was “beating” one of his people. Moses had had enough. He took action. YET. He took the wrong kind of action. He took things into his own hands. He looked around to make sure no one saw what he was about to do. Then he killed the Egyptian and buried him in the sand. He thought he has solved the problem, but he really created one. He had the right heart but the wrong response. You have probably heard the phrase, “two wrongs don’t make a right.” This is an example of it. You have to learn to do the right thing, in the right way. Only then, will you have the moral authority and favor of God to do real justice in the world. Taking things into your own hands out of frustration can create as many problems as it solves. Because Moses hadn’t built a relationship with the people and identified with them, he wasn’t seen as the hero he hoped. The response of his own people is telling here. “Who appointed you to be our prince and judge? Are you going to kill me as you killed that Egyptian yesterday?” If we are really going to learn to help others, we have to discern what is actually helpful for them, not what makes us feel good. We need to discern how to make a difference, not just feel good about it. Romans 10:2 talks about this: “I know what enthusiasm they have for God, but it is misdirected zeal.” Misdirected zeal. You can fire your anger, but miss the target. Moses misdirected his zeal for justice and he had to run away because of it. Moses wanted to set people free, but he was the one who had to flee. "Then Moses was afraid, thinking, “Everyone knows what I did.” And sure enough, Pharaoh heard what had happened, and he tried to kill Moses. But Moses fled from Pharaoh and went to live in the land of Midian. When we try and do the right thing, we have to think about something most people overlook. It’s called the unintended consequence. Zeal without knowledge leads to all sorts of unintended consequences. Intended consequences are what we hope happens. Unintended consequences are the things we don’t think about. Moses meant to set the people free, but he ended up losing his own freedom. As you go through life, seek to do the right thing in the right way. Slow down and ask yourself, what is the wise way to respond to this situation? How do I get the right result, not just the right feeling? This sort of maturity is incredibly rare, and it takes real time to develop. Moses learned the hard way, and you can learn from his life. Wake up, care for others, but act wisely. Keep shifting from self to others, keep stepping in and standing up, but walk with wisdom.
questions: Do you think you are good at handling anger? Why or why not?
How do you think the person you are doing this study with handles anger? Can you give them an example?
Jesus seemed to know how to deal with anger properly. Can you think of an example where he used it well?

thoughts.verse: Understand this, my dear brothers and sisters: You must all be quick to listen, slow to speak, and slow to get angry. Human anger does not produce the righteousness God desires.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you channel your frustration in the right way. Ask him to remind you to pause and think about the unintended consequences of what you might do.

extras: WEEK 3 MANSCHOOL SPONSOR A CHILD MANSCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. SPONSOR A CHILD One of the best ways to help a young guy think about others is by connecting him with the story of someone else his age who has none of the privileges or benefits that he does. Sponsoring a kid is a way to bring that story home. Why not sponsor a kid at the start of this journey and use it as a focal point while you travel the Primal Path together? You may already have a sponsored kid but inviting your son to partner brings him into the story. OPTION 2. WATCH Watch Living on One Dollar https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E9Jy8gKgXi0 A cool 50-minute documentary about 4 young guys who live on one dollar a day in Guatemala. Not super “entertaining,” but could sow some great seeds for a later gap year. OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son out for dinner at one of his favorite spots. Talk with him about the idea of being awakened to others. Recap the week around the 5 devotional topics: Overcoming the "Me Monster" Being compelled by compassion Seeing others Stepping in and standing up God's things, God's way
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
